preamble: Nasalization of the vowel [a] typically occurs through coarticulation with a following nasal consonant, eventually leading to a phonemic nasal vowel after the loss of the consonant.

examples.note: Evolution from Latin 'manus' where the nasal consonant triggered nasalization of the preceding vowel.
Secondary nasalization of vowels in several Bantu languages due to contact with nasal prefixes.

Nasal vowels are a prominent and phonemic feature in many Panoan and Cariban languages of the Amazon basin and lowlands. /a/ exists in both oral and nasal variants as distinct phonemes, representing an inherited proto-feature of these families. This is not coarticulatory nasalization but rather a fundamental part of the vowel system.

Proto-Trans-New Guinea reconstructs nasal vowels as phonemic contrasts alongside oral vowels. Modern Trans-New Guinea languages maintain this distinction, with nasality serving as a distinctive vowel feature similar to oral vs. nasal, representing preservation of an ancient phonemic system.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
